en_title: A Little Gentleness in the Year's Last Days
en_body:
..........HOÀNG-ÂN................................

A Little Gentleness in the Year's Last Days
............................................................

LuiKang Chu-Thanh-Hoàng: First Love cherished and tenderly sustained, so very privately within me..!!
Hoàng my Boundlessly Wondrous Beloved...
 ...In the year's last days, suddenly Heaven and Earth are exquisitely beautiful and warm. Preparing for the year-end feasts and the jubilant gathering to welcome the New Year on New Year's Eve, I sit listening to a recording of homeland music. I am listening to Nguyen Thong's "Remembering the Homeland": the words of the song awaken in me an aching emotion beyond all telling, and make me remember the melodies of national affection so distinctly LuiKang in my Darling Hoàng: I copy down these lyrics, wandering with the heart of one far from home, to send to Hoàng.. with a little trembling and remembrance of my Darling in the lyrical melody so full of longing within the far traveler's heart. The homeland is inside the human heart, and inside those whose spirit is powerful, optimistic, infinitely affirmative: our mountains and rivers and our people are forever carried within us with all thoughts fresh-colored and generous. Yet year's end is also the time we prepare to welcome a Tết far from home: I send Hoàng a little homesick tenderness for the old country!
 ...Now Autumn has faded, space filled with swallows flying home...vanishing behind the thousand mountains...
 Alone, where may I look and see the homeland's form! There, a few thatched roofs, a distant village longing through how many veils
 of mist and smoke! There, the shadows of green leaves, still holding the sound of evening bells falling...
 Living far away alone, my heart aches remembering home! So much affection in the rivers and mountains, why
 does my soul feel so wounded! Sorrowfully remembering the old country, when is my heart ever fresh-colored! When
 I dream and behold the shadow of another's homeland...
 ...Oh, homeland! How much belovedness! Oh, homeland! How much remembrance!
 Though under faraway skies, I shall never forget! The deep-green bamboo shadows, the evening mist
 ascending! Oh, homeland! How much belovedness! Oh, homeland! How much remembrance!
 Faintly far away, the flute sings in the tranquil sky.. Each evening I dream, sitting remembering my homeland!
 ...As the moon slowly rises, rising high above the old bamboo grove. We gather close
 laughing and playing beside the clusters of flowers, or listen deeply to the sad flute-song, dreamlike with love.
 Those peaceful moments have gone far away...
 Everyone's homeland leaves deep impressions of feeling, awakening attachment and longing in the heart of a country's children. Everyone carries love of homeland. I also love America, the place that has sheltered and nourished me and my loved ones for nearly 31 years. This land holds its own endearments for me from my young-growing days, and taught me precious virtues! Yet I remain Vietnamese, with rosy-brown skin and midnight black within my eyes, my hair mingling me among the native people.
 Certain old scenes remain sharply engraved, deeply carved in memory, an old homeland utterly private within me; and precious because they belong to the Past, memories that may live again only through remembrance: the rosy-brown earthen roads of Vietnamese villages: spacious and open, on both sides shadowed by thick tall bamboo ramparts: the bamboo leaves reaching close into deep abundance, long slender leaves with sharp thornlike fibers, when touching one another, their rustling resonance can never be forgotten! Most peaceful were the late deserted noons silent as a page beneath the lazy, faded-golden sunlight of siesta time! Oh, the sound of bamboo singing me to sleep in the homeland wind awakens an inexpressible longing! I remember noons walking to school, the two innocent panels of my white áo dài lingering over the sleek satin trousers, the trembling bamboo-sound as though hiding within my newly washed maiden hair, still fragrant with bồ-kết, grapefruit peel and lemon; or evenings returning late from my maternal grandparents' house, walking between two banks of bamboo swaying wantonly beneath the mystical moonlight: the bamboo whispering its song in waves of wind was truly the resonance of leaf-waves within a dreaming ocean of the village homeland's gentle Universe! Truly it rocked my soul into how many mild and sweet dreams of the rivers and mountains and the Spirit of our people!
 And moonlight glistening wet across the veranda, like a silver river upon the banks of banana leaves gleaming dark blue-green...the rain pattering upon the banana leaves, the house's tin roof, echoing clearly through the wooden window cracks and iron bars, without an isolating pane of glass: the garden with its great old tree trunks, its bushes of flowers and lush leaves immediately beyond the window bars, one need only open the shutters wide to reach and touch the lustrous layers of leaves, gently lift to the face those elegant flowers so simple and rustic with homeland fragrance! There is something full of intimacy and belovedness: bringing human beings near, close beside, as though joining us in dearest kinship with all that is most gentle, tender, and sweet in Nature who nourishes and harbors human life! The gardens of the villages outside Sài-Gòn, where I was born and grew into the sacred age of maidenhood: they wholly cherished and tenderly upheld everything peaceful and quiet within me! Their poetry and lyricism were truly boundless in simplicity and rustic grace: influenced also by the tranquil spirits of laboring people who came from everywhere across the country after 1954, gathering together, building a refuge for life in hope of dwelling peacefully and prospering. From honest and humble dreams yet filled with yearning aspiration, from feet hardened against stone and hands hoping through drops of sweat: together enriching the Future for children and descendants: a refuge for the body, a sanctuary for a gentle life! Those gardens reflected these simple yet wondrous beloved dreams: they sheltered and nourished my innocent childhood days and peaceful maiden years! Though there was grief also for the losses of Father's mourning, for uncles and friends sacrificed to the homeland's dream of Peace: yet in all, I was born and grew in the fortune of never being wounded directly by war and chaos; until April, 1975, my soul remained fresh as the image of those gentle village gardens in memory filled with beautiful and vivid Grace!
 Now we are allowed to continue living peacefully amid the boundless free air of Knowledge and the exhilarating winds of civilized philosophies from distant lands: in these days preparing to welcome a new year with Hope and trembling Joy: I send to my Darling Hoàng a little pure and peaceful aftertaste of a homeland infinitely far away in space, yet intimately near, within the blood of us both!
note: Tết is the Vietnamese lunar New Year, while April 1975 marks the fall of Sài-Gòn and the poet's passage into refugee exile.
